relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Jesus knows the report about growing disciples has reached the Pharisees.
The baptizing work is through the disciples.
He leaves Judea for Galilee.
Samaria is not a detour but necessity.
He comes near Jacob’s field and Joseph’s inheritance.
Wearied by the journey, Jesus sits by the well at the sixth hour.
A Samaritan woman comes for water, and Jesus asks to drink.
The disciples are away seeking food.
She names the boundary: Judean, Samaritan, woman, vessel.
Jesus offers the gift of living water if she knew who asked.
She sees depth and no bucket, yet asks from where living water comes.
Jacob’s well frames her question of greatness.
Ordinary water leaves thirst returning.
His water becomes an inner spring leaping into age-long life.
She asks for the water that ends thirst and repetitive drawing.
Jesus turns the conversation toward truth.
She speaks honestly, and Jesus honors the truth in her answer.
Her history is known without shaming her.
She recognizes prophetic sight.
The question moves from personal history to sacred place.
Jesus announces worship beyond mountain and Jerusalem.
He preserves the Judean stream of salvation without denying the coming expansion.
The hour comes and now is: true worship in Spirit and truth.
The Divine is Spirit; worship must match Spirit and truth.
She waits for Messiah who will announce all things.
Jesus reveals himself directly: I am, the one speaking to you.
The disciples marvel but do not ask the deeper questions.
She leaves the water jar and becomes witness in the city.
Her testimony becomes invitation: come, see.
The city begins moving toward him.
The disciples urge him to eat.
He speaks of food they do not know.
They misunderstand food as literal provision.
His food is doing the Sender’s will and completing the work.
He lifts their eyes to the harvest already white.
Sower and reaper rejoice together in age-long fruit.
The true word: one sows, another reaps.
They enter labor already begun by others.
Many Samaritans trust through the woman’s witness.
They ask him to remain, and he remains two days.
More trust awakens through his word.
They move from her testimony to their own hearing and knowing: Savior of the world.
After two days, he goes to Galilee.
A prophet lacks honor in his homeland.
Galileans receive him after seeing the feast signs.
He returns to Cana, where water became wine; a royal official’s son is sick.
The official asks healing before death.
Jesus exposes sign-dependence.
The official’s need remains urgent: come before my child dies.
The word is enough: your son lives; the man trusts and goes.
Servants meet him with life-news.
The hour of healing is traced.
The father recognizes the word-hour and his whole house trusts.
The second sign completes the Cana-to-Galilee arc.
